# George Van Eps, *Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar*, Vol. 1 — Statement of Outputs

## Overview

Van Eps's *Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar* Vol. 1 is organized around a single architectural polemic: the guitar is not a strummed chord instrument and not a single-line solo instrument, but a *lap piano* — a polyphonic keyboard laid horizontally across the player's lap on which four independent voices move simultaneously under contrapuntal discipline. The book opens by installing that frame and never relaxes it. Every exercise that follows is engineered to produce four-part voice-leading in which outer voices (bass and soprano) function as harmonic anchors while inner voices (alto and tenor) carry the chromatic, contrapuntal motion that makes the texture sound like a keyboard fugue rather than a guitar accompaniment. The load-bearing claim governing the whole method is that harmony on the guitar is not a stack of blocks but a network of simultaneous melodic lines, and that mastering the instrument means mastering the *mechanism* by which those lines move past one another — hence the title. The book is unapologetically engineering-shaped: chord families, string sets, inversions, and voice-leading routes are presented as components of a system, drilled exhaustively, and assembled into polyphonic exercises in which the student must consciously route each of four voices along an independent path.

The pedagogical arc moves in a deliberate sequence from harmonic primitives to polyphonic application, each stage gated on mastery of the previous. The early chapters install the harmonic vocabulary in strict construction order — triads first, then tetrads (seventh chords), then extended chords (9ths, 11ths, 13ths) — grouped by chord-quality category (major, minor, dominant, diminished, augmented, half-diminished) so the student internalizes the family of shapes rather than isolated voicings. Triads are presented in every inversion on every string set; each is then *completed* by adding a fourth voice to convert it to a tetrad, and tetrads are converted to extended chords by substituting an extension (9, 11, 13) for an inner chord tone (typically root or 5th). The middle chapters introduce the inner-voice apparatus: pedal-tone textures in which a held bass or soprano sustains while alto and tenor walk in half-steps, contrary-motion voice pairs that train independence between the hand's outer and inner duties, and chromatic inner-voice motion treated as the connective tissue between any two parent chord positions. The later chapters install the six string-set fingering system — the three adjacent four-string groups (1-2-3-4, 2-3-4-5, 3-4-5-6) plus the three *broken* (non-adjacent) sets that introduce the string-skipping geometry required for genuine polyphony — and run the harmonized scale through each set in turn. The closing chapters assemble the components into explicitly polyphonic exercises: harmonized progressions in which each voice has been pre-routed as its own melodic line, then the student is required to sustain the routing without collapsing back into block-chord finger habits.

Four characteristic Van Eps moves carry the method. First, the *outer-voice anchor with chromatic inner motion*: bass and soprano are held or moved deliberately while alto and tenor advance in half-steps beneath them, producing the harmonic-shimmer-under-pedal-tone signature that distinguishes Van Eps's writing from block-chord comping. Second, the *six fingering forms across string sets*: every harmonized-scale and chord exercise is cycled through all six four-string sets (three adjacent, three broken) before the lesson is considered complete, producing systematic coverage of every voicing in every region of the neck and forcing the student through string-skipping right-hand geometry that ordinary plectrum technique avoids. Third, *pulsation picking*: the right-hand articulation that lets four voices speak with independent rhythmic life rather than collapsing into a chordal stroke; pulsation gives each voice a separable attack so the listener can hear the polyphony. Fourth, *finger-flattening*: the left-hand mechanic by which a single finger bars partial adjacent strings to free other fingers for the inner-voice walks, an engineering accommodation that makes four-part texture physically reachable on a fretboard built for two-hand single-line work.

Taken together, Vol. 1 records an integrated method with two interlocking aesthetic axioms. The first is *counterpoint-as-system*: harmony is not a vertical stack but a horizontal network of independent voices, and every chord progression is, in principle, four simultaneous melodic lines whose paths must be consciously routed. The second is *harmonic-mechanism-as-engineering*: the instrument is treated as a machine whose chord families, string sets, inversions, and voice-leading routes are components to be catalogued, drilled in isolation, and then assembled into working polyphonic textures. The book's spine is therefore not a song list, a substitution menu, or a chord chart but a parts catalog plus an assembly manual — the title's *Mechanisms* is meant literally. Volume 1 covers the harmonic vocabulary, the string-set fingering grid, the inner-voice motion repertoire, and the first round of polyphonic exercises; later volumes extend the apparatus, but Volume 1 establishes that mastery of the guitar consists in mastering the mechanism by which independent voices move past one another, and that every other guitaristic skill — comping, soloing, chord-melody, arranging — is downstream of that contrapuntal foundation.

## Systems

The systems-draft (`s4-systems`) records three load-bearing systems extracted from the chapter summaries: **Inner-Voice Independence (Lap-Piano Polyphony)**, **Six String-Set Fingering System**, and **Four-Part Chord Construction (Triads to Extended Chords)**. The first captures the contrapuntal foundation — outer-voice anchors, chromatic inner motion, pedal-tone textures, contrary-motion voice pairs, and the four-part voice-leading exercises that put them to work. The second captures the fretboard-coverage apparatus — the three adjacent four-string sets, the three broken sets with their string-skipping geometry, the harmonized-scale drill that runs every diatonic chord through one set before changing sets, and the position-preserving fingering shifts that connect sets within a single phrase. The third captures the harmonic vocabulary builder — triad inversions on every string set, the tetrad family (Maj7, mi7, dom7, dim7, half-dim7, aug7) grouped by quality, the extended chords built by substituting an extension for an inner chord tone, and the triad-to-tetrad conversion rule that walks the student from three-voice to four-voice texture without disturbing the outer-voice contour. Each system is constructed so the rules of the others can apply on top of it: the harmonized-scale drill of System 2 runs the chord families of System 3 under the voice-independence constraint of System 1.

## Pending Work

The systems-draft carries `_pending:` engine_payload kinds for the two voice-leading moves that are characteristically Van Eps and not yet covered by the existing engine vocabulary:

- `_pending:outer-voice-anchor` — the rule that bass and soprano are sustained as harmonic anchors while inner voices carry the chromatic motion; no existing kind expresses the outer-voice-pedal-with-inner-walk pattern as a first-class operation.
- `_pending:chromatic-inner-motion` — the rule that alto and tenor advance by half-step or pass through chromatic neighbors beneath stable outer voices; the existing `VoiceMotion` kind captures the general voice-leading principle but does not distinguish the inner-voice-only chromatic-walk pattern that is Van Eps's signature.

A larger set of rules map cleanly to existing engine kinds: `VoiceMotion` (each voice as an independent line; chromatic-approach inner embellishment), `StringSetTransition` (cycling through all six string sets; harmonizing the scale on one set before changing), `PositionContinuity` (broken-set fingerings; preserving left-hand position across set changes), `FamilyCoherence` (triads-before-tetrads-before-extensions order; grouping voicings by chord-quality category), and `SubstitutionExpand` (extension substituting for an inner chord tone; triad-to-tetrad conversion by adding a fourth voice).

Two characteristic Van Eps moves named in the prose — *pulsation picking* and *finger-flattening* — appear in the book's text but did not surface as standalone traversal rules in the s4-systems draft. Both are right-hand and left-hand mechanical accommodations that serve the polyphony rather than harmonic rules per se; they would land naturally as `_pending:` engine kinds (`_pending:pulsation-picking`, `_pending:finger-flattening`) if promoted from prose into the rule layer in a later pass.

## Provenance Notes

All system blocks and rules are drawn from the book-level distillation (`s3-book.response.json`) and the fourteen per-chapter summaries (`s3-chapter-ch01` through `s3-chapter-ch14`). The s4-systems draft (`s4-systems.response.json`) carries `references[]` on each rule pointing back to specific chapters and topics in those summaries (e.g., the lap-piano concept and outer-voice anchor at ch01; chromatic inner-voice motion at ch02; six string-set fingerings at ch04; harmonized-scale practice at ch05; broken-set fingerings at ch06; sequential triad-tetrad-extension order and quality grouping at ch07; extensions-as-substitution-expansions at ch08).

The three systems map cleanly to the book's pedagogical arc. The Four-Part Chord Construction system captures the early chapters' harmonic-vocabulary build (chord families, inversions, triad-to-tetrad-to-extension order). The Six String-Set Fingering System captures the middle chapters' fretboard-coverage apparatus (adjacent and broken sets, harmonized-scale drill, position-preserving transitions). The Inner-Voice Independence system captures the contrapuntal apparatus introduced in the opening chapters and applied throughout the rest of the book (outer-voice anchors, chromatic inner motion, pedal-tone textures, contrary-motion voice pairs, four-part voice-leading exercises). No chapter failed to yield a system contribution, though the polyphonic-exercise chapters at the end of the volume primarily *apply* the three systems rather than introducing new structural rules.

## Scope and Source Caveats

Three caveats are load-bearing for any reader of this statement. First, *Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar* Vol. 1 is George Van Eps's seven-string method. Van Eps designed and played a seven-string guitar (the low A string added below the standard six-string tuning), and the inner-voice apparatus, the six string-set fingerings, and the pedal-tone textures of System 1 all assume the seven-string range. Six-string players can apply most of the harmonic content (chord families, triad inversions, quality categories, extension substitutions) directly, but the four-string-set geometry and several of the polyphonic exercises presuppose the extra bass string; readers transposing the method onto six-string instruments must accept either a re-fingered subset or a compressed register. This is a load-bearing scope constraint and is not captured by any rule in the systems-draft because it is a property of the instrument, not of the harmony.

Second, this distillation is derived from an OCR-sourced transcript of a 334-page print volume that mixes prose, chord-diagram graphics, and standard-notation engravings, processed via the *cyberpower* OCR pipeline. The book-level distillation and the systems-draft were assembled from the per-chapter summaries that re-encode the verbal content of the prose; the chord-diagram and music-notation graphics are not directly captured by the OCR text channel, and any specific voicing claims in this statement (the six string-set partitions; the triad-inversion-per-set drill; the harmonized-scale routine; the chromatic-walk-under-pedal-tone pattern) trace through the chapter-summary prose rather than through a separate verification against the original engraved plates. Readers consuming this statement as a basis for engine-layer promotion should treat the verbal claims as authoritative for the method's *structure* and treat the specific voicing spellings, fingering geometries, and string-set membership claims as subject to confirmation against the printed diagrams.

Third, Volume 1 is the first installment of a multi-volume method. The contrapuntal apparatus is fully installed in Volume 1, but the polyphonic-application repertoire — extended exercises, arrangements, and chord-melody pieces that exercise the mechanism on real tunes — is distributed across later volumes. The statement above describes what Volume 1 establishes and does not claim coverage of material reserved for subsequent volumes.
